summary:
The Viceroyalty of New Spain (1535–1821 AD) was the largest and most important of Spain's colonial territories — governing a vast area from modern California and the American Southwest through Mexico and Central America to the Philippines and the Caribbean, administered from the capital Mexico City (built atop the ruins of the [[event:2319|destroyed Aztec capital]] Tenochtitlán). Established in 1535 with Antonio de Mendoza as the first viceroy, New Spain became the administrative, economic, and cultural center of Spain's American empire: the silver mines of Zacatecas and Guanajuato (discovered in the 1540s-1550s) replaced the exhausted Peruvian mines as Spain's primary source of precious metals, and Mexico City became one of the world's largest cities by 1600.

New Spain's cultural legacy was complex and enduring. The forced labor and epidemic disease of the early colonial period killed an estimated 90% of Mexico's Indigenous population within a century of the conquest — one of history's greatest demographic catastrophes. The Catholic Church established universities (the Royal and Pontifical University of Mexico, 1551), hospitals, and missions throughout the viceroyalty, while Indigenous cultural and religious traditions survived in syncretic fusion with Catholicism. The colonial [[event:3285|mission system]] in California and Texas spread Spanish culture northward. By the late 18th century, creole identity — the self-awareness of American-born Spaniards as distinct from peninsulares — had developed alongside growing Enlightenment-influenced criticism of colonial governance, setting the stage for Miguel Hidalgo's Grito de Dolores (1810) that began the Mexican War of Independence.
